Introduction 1.1 About This Course 1.2 How to Use This Book 1.3 Prerequisites 1.4 Contact

Introduction Welcome to the course materials for Intermediate Microeconomics. This book serves as a central hub for lecture notes, problem sets, supplemental readings, and other resources related to the course. 1.1 About This Course Intermediate Microeconomics builds on the foundations of introductory economics to develop a more rigorous and mathematical framework for understanding how individuals, firms, and markets make decisions. Topics covered include: Consumer Theory: How individuals make choices given preferences and budget constraints Producer Theory: How firms choose inputs and outputs to maximize profit Market Structure: Competitive markets, monopoly, oligopoly, and game theory General Equilibrium: How markets interact and reach economy-wide equilibrium 1.2 How to Use This Book The chapters in this book follow the general progression of the course. Each chapter contains: Lecture Notes: Key concepts and derivations from class Examples: Worked examples to illustrate the theory Practice Problems: Problems to test your understanding (solutions may be provided separately) Use the table of contents on the left to navigate between chapters. The Resources chapter at the end contains links to external materials that may be helpful throughout the course. 1.3 Prerequisites Students should be comfortable with: Introductory Microeconomics (supply, demand, basic market analysis) Calculus (derivatives, partial derivatives, constrained optimization) Basic algebra and graphing 1.4 Contact Please refer to the course syllabus for office hours, contact information, and course policies.

Chapter 2 Consumer Theory Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 2.1 Overview Consumer theory provides a rigorous framework for understanding how individuals make choices. We model consumers as rational agents who maximize utility subject to a budget constraint. Key questions: What does it mean for a consumer to be “rational”? How do we represent preferences mathematically? How does a consumer choose the best affordable bundle? How do changes in prices and income affect consumption? 2.2 Topics Preference relations and their properties (completeness, transitivity, monotonicity, convexity) Utility functions as representations of preferences Budget sets and budget constraints Utility maximization The Marshallian (ordinary) demand function Comparative statics: income and substitution effects The Slutsky equation Compensated (Hicksian) demand and expenditure minimization Duality between utility maximization and expenditure minimization Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.
Chapter 3 Budget Constraint 3.1 Overview 3.2 Topics 3.3 Key Formula

Chapter 3 Budget Constraint Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 3.1 Overview Before a consumer can choose what to consume, we need to understand what they can consume. The budget constraint defines the set of feasible consumption bundles given prices and income. 3.2 Topics The budget set and budget line Equation of the budget line: \(p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = m\) Intercepts and slope of the budget line Changes in income: parallel shifts of the budget line Changes in prices: rotating the budget line Ad valorem taxes and quantity taxes Quantity subsidies and lump-sum subsidies Rationing and other non-standard budget sets 3.3 Key Formula The budget line for two goods with prices \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) and income \(m\): \[p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = m\] Rearranging to slope-intercept form: \[x_2 = \frac{m}{p_2} - \frac{p_1}{p_2} x_1\] The slope of the budget line is \(-p_1/p_2\), which represents the opportunity cost of good 1 in terms of good 2. Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.
Chapter 4 Preferences and Utility 4.1 Overview 4.2 Topics

Chapter 4 Preferences and Utility Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 4.1 Overview Consumer preferences describe how individuals rank different consumption bundles. Utility functions provide a mathematical representation of these preferences. 4.2 Topics 4.2.1 Preferences Preference relations: strict preference (\(\succ\)), weak preference (\(\succsim\)), indifference (\(\sim\)) Assumptions on preferences: completeness, transitivity Desirable properties: monotonicity (more is better), convexity Indifference curves: graphical representation of preferences Marginal rate of substitution (MRS) 4.2.2 Utility Functions Ordinal vs. cardinal utility When does a utility function represent preferences? Common utility functions: Perfect substitutes: \(u(x_1, x_2) = ax_1 + bx_2\) Perfect complements: \(u(x_1, x_2) = \min\{ax_1, bx_2\}\) Cobb-Douglas: \(u(x_1, x_2) = x_1^a x_2^b\) Quasilinear: \(u(x_1, x_2) = v(x_1) + x_2\) Monotonic transformations of utility Computing and interpreting the MRS from a utility function Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.
Chapter 5 Consumer Choice 5.1 Overview 5.2 Topics 5.3 The Lagrangian Method

Chapter 5 Consumer Choice Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 5.1 Overview Consumer choice brings together the budget constraint and preferences to determine the optimal consumption bundle — the bundle that maximizes utility given the consumer’s income and the prices they face. 5.2 Topics The utility maximization problem (UMP) Interior vs. corner solutions The tangency condition: \(\text{MRS} = p_1/p_2\) Solving for optimal demand using calculus (Lagrangian method) Special cases: Perfect substitutes (corner solutions) Perfect complements (kink solutions) Cobb-Douglas (interior solution) Quasilinear preferences The indirect utility function \(v(p_1, p_2, m)\) Roy’s Identity 5.3 The Lagrangian Method To solve \(\max_{x_1, x_2} u(x_1, x_2)\) subject to \(p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = m\): Set up the Lagrangian: \[\mathcal{L} = u(x_1, x_2) - \lambda(p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 - m)\] First-order conditions: \[\frac{\partial u}{\partial x_1} = \lambda p_1, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x_2} = \lambda p_2, \quad p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = m\] Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 6 Demand Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 6.1 Overview This chapter examines how consumer demand responds to changes in prices and income, and introduces the formal tools for analyzing these relationships. 6.2 Topics 6.2.1 Demand Functions and Curves Marshallian (ordinary) demand: \(x_i^*(p_1, p_2, m)\) Demand curves and inverse demand curves Normal goods, inferior goods, and Giffen goods 6.2.2 Income and Substitution Effects How a price change affects demand through two channels: Substitution effect: the change in demand due to a change in relative prices (holding utility constant) Income effect: the change in demand due to a change in real purchasing power The Slutsky decomposition The Slutsky equation: \(\frac{\partial x_i}{\partial p_j} = \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial p_j} - x_j \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial m}\) 6.2.3 Compensated (Hicksian) Demand The expenditure minimization problem (EMP) Hicksian demand: \(h_i(p_1, p_2, u)\) The expenditure function: \(e(p_1, p_2, u)\) Shephard’s lemma: \(\frac{\partial e}{\partial p_i} = h_i\) 6.2.4 Welfare Analysis Consumer surplus Compensating variation (CV) and equivalent variation (EV) Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 7 Production Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 7.1 Overview Production theory analyzes how firms transform inputs (labor, capital, materials) into outputs. Just as consumer theory models rational consumers, production theory models profit-maximizing firms. 7.2 Topics 7.2.1 Production Functions The production function: \(q = f(L, K)\) Inputs and outputs Short run vs. long run Marginal product of labor (\(MP_L\)) and capital (\(MP_K\)) Average product of labor Law of diminishing marginal returns 7.2.2 Isoquants Isoquants as the firm’s equivalent of indifference curves Marginal rate of technical substitution (MRTS): \(MRTS = MP_L / MP_K\) Returns to scale: constant, increasing, and decreasing CRS: \(f(tL, tK) = t \cdot f(L, K)\) IRS: \(f(tL, tK) > t \cdot f(L, K)\) DRS: \(f(tL, tK) < t \cdot f(L, K)\) 7.2.3 Common Production Functions Cobb-Douglas: \(f(L, K) = L^\alpha K^\beta\) Perfect substitutes: \(f(L, K) = aL + bK\) Perfect complements (Leontief): \(f(L, K) = \min\{aL, bK\}\) Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 8 Costs Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 8.1 Overview Cost theory translates production possibilities into monetary terms. Understanding costs is essential for analyzing firm behavior and market outcomes. 8.2 Topics 8.2.1 Cost Concepts Opportunity cost and economic cost vs. accounting cost Sunk costs Fixed costs (FC) and variable costs (VC) Total cost: \(TC = FC + VC\) 8.2.2 Cost Minimization The cost minimization problem: \(\min_{L,K} wL + rK\) subject to \(f(L,K) = q\) Isocost lines: \(wL + rK = C\) Optimality condition: \(MRTS = w/r\) (i.e., \(MP_L/MP_K = w/r\)) Conditional factor demands: \(L^*(w, r, q)\), \(K^*(w, r, q)\) The cost function: \(c(w, r, q)\) Shephard’s lemma for costs 8.2.3 Short-Run vs. Long-Run Costs Short-run cost curves: MC, AVC, ATC, AFC Long-run cost curves and the envelope relationship Economies and diseconomies of scale Minimum efficient scale 8.2.4 Key Relationships Average total cost (ATC) = TC/q Average variable cost (AVC) = VC/q Marginal cost (MC) = \(\partial TC / \partial q\) MC crosses AVC and ATC at their minimum points Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 9 Profit Maximization Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 9.1 Overview How do firms decide how much to produce? This chapter examines profit maximization — the central behavioral assumption for firms — and its implications for supply decisions. 9.2 Topics 9.2.1 Profit Maximization Profit: \(\pi = R(q) - C(q) = pq - c(q)\) The profit-maximizing condition: \(MR = MC\) For a price-taking firm: \(p = MC\) The supply function of a competitive firm The profit function: \(\pi^*(p, w, r)\) 9.2.2 Short-Run Supply Shut-down decision: produce if \(p \geq AVC\) The short-run supply curve Producer surplus 9.2.3 Long-Run Supply Long-run shut-down decision: produce if \(p \geq ATC\) Zero-profit condition in long-run equilibrium The long-run supply curve of the firm 9.2.4 Duality and the Profit Function Hotelling’s lemma: \(\frac{\partial \pi^*}{\partial p} = q^*\) (output supply) \(\frac{\partial \pi^*}{\partial w} = -L^*\) (labor demand) The profit function is convex in prices Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 10 Competitive Markets Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 10.1 Overview This chapter analyzes perfectly competitive markets, where many buyers and sellers interact, and no individual agent can influence the market price. We examine how competitive markets reach equilibrium and evaluate their efficiency properties. 10.2 Topics 10.2.1 Market Structure Characteristics of perfect competition: Many buyers and sellers Homogeneous product Free entry and exit Perfect information Price-taking behavior 10.2.2 Short-Run Equilibrium Market supply curve (horizontal sum of firm supply curves) Short-run equilibrium: \(Q^D(p) = Q^S(p)\) Comparative statics: shifts in supply and demand Short-run producer surplus and welfare 10.2.3 Long-Run Equilibrium Free entry drives profits to zero Long-run equilibrium: \(p = \min(ATC)\) Long-run market supply curve Constant-cost, increasing-cost, and decreasing-cost industries 10.2.4 Welfare Analysis Consumer surplus and producer surplus Deadweight loss from taxes and price controls The First Welfare Theorem: competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 11 Monopoly Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 11.1 Overview A monopoly is a market with a single seller who has market power — the ability to set prices above marginal cost. This chapter analyzes monopoly behavior and its welfare consequences. 11.2 Topics 11.2.1 Monopoly Behavior Sources of market power: barriers to entry, patents, natural monopoly The monopolist’s demand curve (downward sloping) Revenue: \(R(q) = p(q) \cdot q\) Marginal revenue: \(MR = p + q \cdot \frac{dp}{dq} < p\) Profit maximization: \(MR = MC\) Lerner Index of market power: \(\frac{p - MC}{p} = -\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\) 11.2.2 Monopoly Welfare Deadweight loss from monopoly Comparison with competitive outcome Efficiency and equity concerns 11.2.3 Price Discrimination First-degree (perfect) price discrimination Second-degree price discrimination (nonlinear pricing, two-part tariffs) Third-degree price discrimination (market segmentation) Condition: \(MR_1 = MR_2 = MC\) Higher price in the market with less elastic demand 11.2.4 Natural Monopoly and Regulation Natural monopoly: subadditive costs Regulation: marginal cost pricing vs. average cost pricing Rate-of-return regulation Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 12 Game Theory Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 12.1 Overview Game theory provides a framework for analyzing strategic interactions — situations where the outcome for each agent depends on the actions of others. It is essential for understanding oligopoly markets and many other economic settings. 12.2 Topics 12.2.1 Static Games Players, strategies, and payoffs Normal form (matrix) representation Dominant strategies and iterated elimination of dominated strategies Nash equilibrium: no player wants to deviate unilaterally Nash equilibrium in pure and mixed strategies Examples: Prisoner’s Dilemma, Battle of the Sexes, Matching Pennies 12.2.2 Dynamic Games Extensive form (tree) representation Sequential vs. simultaneous moves Backward induction Subgame perfect Nash equilibrium (SPNE) Examples: Stackelberg duopoly, ultimatum game, entry deterrence 12.2.3 Repeated Games Finitely vs. infinitely repeated games The Folk Theorem: cooperation can be sustained in infinitely repeated games Discount factors and patience 12.2.4 Applications Coordination games and focal points Auctions as games Signaling games and adverse selection Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 13 Oligopoly Coming soon — lecture notes and materials for this chapter will be added as the course progresses. 13.1 Overview Oligopoly markets are characterized by a small number of strategically interacting firms. Unlike perfect competition or monopoly, the outcomes in oligopoly depend on the specific model of strategic behavior firms are assumed to adopt. 13.2 Topics 13.2.1 Models of Oligopoly 13.2.1.1 Cournot Competition (Quantity Competition) Firms simultaneously choose quantities Best response functions Cournot-Nash equilibrium Comparison with monopoly and competitive outcomes With \(n\) firms: as \(n \to \infty\), Cournot approaches competitive outcome 13.2.1.2 Bertrand Competition (Price Competition) Firms simultaneously choose prices Bertrand paradox: with homogeneous goods and two firms, \(p = MC\) Resolution: capacity constraints, differentiated products, repeated interaction 13.2.1.3 Stackelberg Leadership Sequential quantity competition Leader moves first; follower best-responds Solved by backward induction Leader produces more than in Cournot; follower produces less 13.2.2 Collusion Incentives for firms to collude (joint profit maximization) Instability of collusion: incentive to deviate Collusion as a repeated game equilibrium 13.2.3 Product Differentiation Differentiated Bertrand competition Hotelling model of spatial competition Detailed notes will be posted here as the semester progresses.

Chapter 14 Resources 14.1 Textbooks 14.2 Lecture Notes 14.3 Video Lectures 14.4 Mathematical Tools 14.5 Problem Sets and Practice 14.6 Data and Empirical Applications 14.7 Online Encyclopedias and References 14.8 R and Reproducible Research

Chapter 14 Resources This chapter collects links to useful external resources for Intermediate Microeconomics. These include free textbooks, lecture notes, video lectures, mathematical tools, and data sources. 14.1 Textbooks 14.1.1 Primary Varian, Hal R. — Intermediate Microeconomics: A Modern Approach (W.W. Norton) The standard undergraduate intermediate micro textbook. A companion Workouts book provides additional problems. 14.1.2 Free / Open-Access Textbooks OpenStax Microeconomics — A free, peer-reviewed introductory/intermediate textbook. Principles of Economics (Libretexts) — Open-access economics textbooks and notes. Varian — Microeconomic Analysis — The graduate-level companion to Varian’s undergraduate text (advanced reference). 14.2 Lecture Notes MIT OpenCourseWare — 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics — Lecture notes and problem sets from MIT’s introductory micro course. MIT OpenCourseWare — 14.03 Microeconomic Theory and Public Policy — Intermediate-level notes. Penn State — ECON 301 Lecture Notes — Freely available lecture notes for intermediate micro. 14.3 Video Lectures Khan Academy — Microeconomics — Short video lessons covering core micro concepts. Marginal Revolution University — Free video-based micro course by Tyler Cowen and Alex Tabarrok. Yale — ECON 159: Game Theory (Ben Polak) — Full lecture series on game theory from Yale’s Open Yale Courses. 14.4 Mathematical Tools Paul’s Online Math Notes — Calculus — Excellent free reference for calculus, including partial derivatives and optimization. Math for Economists (Simon & Blume) — A standard math methods textbook for economics students. Khan Academy — Multivariable Calculus — Free videos on partial derivatives, gradients, and Lagrange multipliers. 14.5 Problem Sets and Practice Varian Workouts in Intermediate Microeconomics — The standard problem book accompanying Varian’s text. Economics Network — Problem Sets — Resources for economics students and educators in the UK. 14.6 Data and Empirical Applications FRED Economic Data (St. Louis Fed) — Macroeconomic and microeconomic data series. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Data on prices, employment, wages, and more. Census Bureau — American Community Survey — Household and individual-level data for empirical applications. 14.7 Online Encyclopedias and References Investopedia — Economics — Plain-language explanations of economic concepts. The Library of Economics and Liberty (Econlib) — Encyclopedia of Economics and Liberty, including The Concise Encyclopedia of Economics. 14.8 R and Reproducible Research Since this book is built with R Markdown and bookdown, students interested in the tools used are encouraged to explore: bookdown: Authoring Books and Technical Documents with R Markdown — The official bookdown documentation. R for Data Science — Free online book for learning R and data analysis. RStudio — The standard IDE for R.
